coredata.dc:title: Formation mechanism of lignin-derived carbon quantum dots: From chemical structures to fluorescent behaviors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Lingli
Wu, Hao
Yang, Hongmin
Shen, Dekui
Hu, Haitao
Dou, Mengjia
coredata.dc:description: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantage of being green and low-cost, but their complex structure makes the study of their formation mechanism encounter a bottleneck. Lignin-derived CQDs were prepared by a two-step process of “low-temperature liquid depolymerization” coupled with “hydrothermal reaction” in a mild organic acid system. In the first step of the low-temperature acidolysis process, the lignin polymer first undergoes deethering and depolymerization reactions. During the hydrothermal process in the second step, the organic small molecules on the surface of the supernatant are enriched with reactive groups that are dehydrated, condensed, crosslinked, and carbonized under high temperature and pressure to form CQDs. On the other hand, these activated large sp2 carbon domains in the oxidized solid residue from lignin acidolysis undergo hydrothermal cleavage under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, followed by deoxygenation and eventual decomposition into small carbon domain CQDs products. Among them, the supernatant component C1 after lignin acidolysis with abundant N-H and C-OH reactive groups is targeted as a key precursor for the formation of lignin-derived CQDs, and the resulting CQDs have both the highest QY (19.5%) and yield (16.5%). This study bridges the research gap on the formation mechanism of biomass-derived CQDs and offers a reference for the sustainable preparation of biomass-derived CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Two-step process
Formation mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Acidolysis of lignin and molecular weight cut
Physicochemical properties of the components of lignin acidolysis
Molecular weight distribution of different components
Chemical structure characteristics of the different components
Optical properties of CQDs from components of lignin acidolysis
Structural characteristics of the CQDs from components of lignin acidolysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 52376172 and 52406250), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant BK20240592), the College Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant 24KJB480011), the Key Research & Development Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant BE2020114) and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Extreme Conditions (Grant 2023B1212010002). The authors would like to thank Zhao Jie from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TPRL analysis.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 52376172 ), the Key Research & Development Program of Jiangsu Province (grant number: BE2020114) and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Extreme Conditions (grant number: 2023B1212010002). The authors would like to thank Zhao Jie from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TPRL analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The two-step process for the synthesis of CQDs from AL by low-temperature depolymerization coupled with a hydrothermal reaction.
The molecular weight distributions of (a) AL, (b) C1, (c) C2, and (d) C3.
(a) Raman spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) XPS survey spectra, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) N 1s of lignin and different lignin components.

(a) TEM and HR-TEM images (insert: size distribution of CQDs), (b) HR-MS spectra, (c) UV‒vis absorption spectra, PL spectra (insert: fluorescence images of the CQD aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light), and (d) TRPL decay curves of different CQDs.
(a) Raman spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) XPS survey spectra, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) N 1s of different CQDs.
2D-HSQC NMR spectra of (a) AL, (b) C1–C5, and (c) CQDs-1–CQDs-5.
Possible formation pathways in the two-step process of lignin-derived CQDs.
Quantitative analysis results of the XPS data.
Fitting parameters of the TRPL decay curves for different CQDs.
QYs and yields of different CQDs.
Quantitative analysis results of the XPS data for the different CQDs.
Linkage bond integrals for lignin, acidolysis components and CQDs.

head.ce:title: Formation mechanism of lignin-derived carbon quantum dots: From chemical structures to fluorescent behaviors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Extreme Conditions, Dongguan 523803, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Extreme Conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Extreme Conditions, Dongguan 523803, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantage of being green and low-cost, but their complex structure makes the study of their formation mechanism encounter a bottleneck. Lignin-derived CQDs were prepared by a two-step process of “low-temperature liquid depolymerization” coupled with “hydrothermal reaction” in a mild organic acid system. In the first step of the low-temperature acidolysis process, the lignin polymer first undergoes deethering and depolymerization reactions. During the hydrothermal process in the second step, the organic small molecules on the surface of the supernatant are enriched with reactive groups that are dehydrated, condensed, crosslinked, and carbonized under high temperature and pressure to form CQDs. On the other hand, these activated large sp2 carbon domains in the oxidized solid residue from lignin acidolysis undergo hydrothermal cleavage under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, followed by deoxygenation and eventual decomposition into small carbon domain CQDs products. Among them, the supernatant component C1 after lignin acidolysis with abundant N-H and C-OH reactive groups is targeted as a key precursor for the formation of lignin-derived CQDs, and the resulting CQDs have both the highest QY (19.5%) and yield (16.5%). This study bridges the research gap on the formation mechanism of biomass-derived CQDs and offers a reference for the sustainable preparation of biomass-derived CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs, which were discovered in 2004 as a new class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, are usually monodisperse quasispherical carbon nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm consisting of sp2/sp3 carbon cores and surface functional groups (Ai et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017). CQDs are commonly prepared by “top-down” (arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical, etc.) and “bottom-up” (hydrothermal, microwave, ultrasonic, pyrolysis, etc.) methods. Among them, the hydrothermal method is considered to be an effective route for the green synthesis of CQDs from lignin due to the use of water as the reaction medium and mild reaction conditions (Abbas et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020). The hydrothermal method has the advantages of simple operation, easy control of reaction conditions, high product purity, fewer byproducts and less pollution, so it has greater environmental and economic benefits (Liu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2018). The hydrothermal synthesis of lignin CQDs can be generally divided into two types: “one-pot” and “two-step” approaches. Although the one-pot approach is simple but has low controllability, few reports have described the preparation of lignin-derived CQDs with excellent optical properties. The fluorescence quantum yield of lignin-derived CQDs prepared by Niu et al. using the “one-pot method” was only 1.68 % (Niu et al., 2017).

The “two-step” approach involves depolymerizing the lignin raw materials in the liquid phase (pretreatment) in the first step and hydrothermal transformation of the liquid-phase product from the pretreatment step in the second step, resulting in CQDs with outstanding optical properties (Ding et al., 2018b; Gao et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019). The “two step” approach is currently the mainstream method for the preparation of CQDs from lignin. After ultrasonic pretreatment (2 h) coupled with a hydrothermal reaction, Gao et al. obtained blue CQDs with an average particle size of 3.17 nm and a QY of 17.3 % by mixing alkali lignin and concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 (3:1, V/V) (Gao et al., 2021). Ding et al. prepared lignin-derived CQDs on a gram scale via a “two-step” process of HNO3 depolymerization coupled with hydrothermal treatment by prolonged (12 h) sonication. The yield and QY of the obtained blue‒green CQDs were ∼21.0 % and 21 %, respectively (Ding et al., 2018b). Aruna et al. obtained blue CQDs with a QY of 23 % by using an ultrasonic (12 h) pretreatment coupled with a hydrothermal reaction of lignosulfonate sodium salt with concentrated HNO3 (Nair et al., 2020). The optical properties of lignin-derived CQDs prepared by the “two-step” method are considerably improved compared to those of the “one-pot” method. Nevertheless, most of the currently reported “two-step” processes use corrosive acids (HNO3, H2SO4) in the first step, which has drawbacks such as high cost, complex processes, harsh reaction conditions and high energy consumption (Wang et al., 2021c; Wang et al., 2019). Therefore, more environmentally friendly solvents and mild depolymerization are urgently needed to achieve the green preparation of lignin-derived CQDs. Moreover, the reaction intermediates are difficult to separate in a one-pot process, whereas they are comparatively easy to separate in a two-step process for the generation of lignin-derived CQDs. However, as an amorphous three-dimensional polymer, lignin has a wide variety of liquid phase products and complex structures after depolymerization pretreatment, which makes the structures of the key intermediates for further hydrothermal generation of CQDs unidentified, hindering the substantial improvement of the QYs of lignin-based CQDs. It is necessary to adopt new research methods for structural characterization of key intermediates to determine the direction of efficient and sustainable preparation of lignin-derived CQDs.

In this work, lignin-derived CQDs were prepared by a two-step process of “low-temperature liquid depolymerization” coupled with “hydrothermal reaction” in a mild organic acid system. A “molecular weight cut” separation method was established for the first step of the liquid acid hydrolysis of lignin products by applying the dialysis principle. The distribution patterns of the liquid-phase products of the low-temperature depolymerization process of lignin in each group were analyzed. The key precursors for the generation of lignin-derived CQDs were targeted. The mapping relationship between the optical properties of the lignin CQDs and the chemical structure of the precursors was revealed, and the formation mechanism of the lignin-derived CQDs was elucidated.
The materials and chemicals used are detailed in supporting materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acidolysis of lignin and molecular weight cut
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , 0.8 g of alkali lignin (AL) and 0.6 g of o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid were slowly added to a beaker containing 30 mL of deionized water with constant stirring. The mixed solution was heated in a water bath at 70 °C for 1 h with continuous stirring at a rate of 350 rpm. The mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature after the reaction. Then, the acidolysis products were centrifuged at a high speed of 8000 rpm for 10 min for preliminary solid‒liquid separation. The upper clear liquid (SL) and solid residue (SR) were separated by vacuum filtration through a 1.2 μm microporous filter. They were then subjected to further molecular weight cuts individually. The supernatant obtained from lignin acidolysis was first dialyzed in a 5000 Da dialysis bag in deionized water. The deionized water was changed every 6 h until solution A was removed from the bag. The solution A outside the bag was collected and freeze-dried at −60 ℃ for 48 h to obtain the component ① (≤5000 Da) of lignin acidolysis. The solution B inside the bag was transferred to a 50,000 Da dialysis bag in deionized water for dialysis, and the deionized water was changed every 6 h until solution C outside the bag was clarified. The solution C outside the bag was collected and freeze-dried at −60 ℃ for 48 h to obtain component ② (5000–50000 Da) of lignin acidolysis. The solution D inside the bag was collected and freeze-dried to obtain component ③ (≥50,000 Da) for lignin acidolysis. The solid residue obtained from lignin acidolysis was added to an appropriate amount of deionized water and subjected to 0.8 μm microporous vacuum filtration. The separated filtrate was collected and freeze-dried to obtain component ④ (0.8–1.2 μm) for lignin acidolysis. The separated solid was collected and freeze-dried to obtain component ⑤ (≥1.2 μm) after lignin acidolysis. The separated components ①–⑤ of lignin acidolysis are denoted as C1, C2, C3, C4, and C5, respectively.

The resulting different components, C1–C5, were transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The reacted solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and vacuum filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE microporous filter membrane. The obtained filtrate was dialyzed in a 3000 Da dialysis bag in deionized water to remove unreacted small molecules, and the deionized water was changed every 6 h until the solution outside the bag was clarified. The dialyzed CQDs solution was freeze-dried at −60 ℃ for 48 h, after which the obtained CQDs powder was collected. Among them, the components C1–C5 of lignin acidolysis were hydrothermally obtained as five lignin-derived CQDs, denoted as CQDs-1, CQDs-2, CQDs-3, CQDs-4 and CQDs-5, respectively. The yield of the CQDs was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Yield
s
w t %
=
W
CQDs
W
AL
× 100 %
where
Yield
s
represents the yield of the CQDs and
W
AL
,
W
CQDs
represent the mass of the feedstock lignin and the resulting CQDs, respectively.
The characterization of the composite photocatalysts is detailed in supporting materials.
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) measurements are detailed in supporting materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the components of lignin acidolysis
Molecular weight distribution of different components
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular weight distribution of lignin and its components after acidolysis was analyzed using GPC and HR-MS characterization. The lignin acidolysis products at low-temperature were mainly separated into supernatants and solid residues. The upper clear liquid was further separated into component C1 (≤5000 Da), component C2 (5000–50,000 Da) and component C3 (≥50,000 Da) using the molecular weight cut method. The solid residue was separated into component C4 (0.8–1.2 μm) and component C5 (≥1.2 μm). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the number average molecular weight (Mn) of lignin is 2041 g·mol−1, the weight average molecular weight (Mw) is 5501 g·mol−1, and the molecular weight dispersion index (PDI=Mw/Mn) is 2.70. The number average molecular weights (Mn) of the solid residue components C4 and C5 after lignin acidolysis were 1614 and 1823 g·mol−1, the weight average molecular weights (Mw) were 2835 and 3327 g·mol−1, and the molecular weight dispersion indices (PDI=Mw/Mn) were 1.75 and 1.83, respectively. The molecular weight and molecular weight dispersion index of the solid residue components C4 and C5 decreased after lignin acidolysis. This indicates that the lignin depolymerized to a certain extent and formed a solid product of high polymers. As shown in Fig. 2b-d, the molecular weight of the supernatant exhibited a maximum abundance up to 300 m/z, and the abundance gradually decreased with increasing molecular weight. The molecular weight distribution of component C1 was mainly concentrated in the range of 100–300 m/z, with significantly stronger abundances at 128, 151, 172, 187, 212 and 235 m/z. The molecular weight distribution of component C2 was mainly concentrated in the range of 300–600 m/z, with significantly stronger abundances at 307, 362, 434, 459, 498 and 535 m/z. The molecular weight distribution of component C3 was mainly concentrated in the range of 600–1000 m/z, with significantly stronger abundances at 663, 695, 720, 769, 742, 775 and 838 m/z. The supernatant from lignin acidolysis is separated into oligomeric components C1, C2 and C3 by molecular weight cut, and their molecular weights increase sequentially. Furthermore, the HR SEM image of lignin in Fig. S1 (see supplementary materials) shows that the original lignin structure is dense and lumpy, confirming the depolymerization of lignin during acidolysis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure characteristics of the different components
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structural properties of lignin and different components (C1–C5) were analyzed using Raman, FT-IR and XPS characterization. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the Raman spectra of lignin and components C4 and C5 all show two distinct peaks at 1330–1420 and 1582–1633 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G peaks, respectively. The G peak represents sp2 graphitic carbon in the structure of the substance, while the D peak represents sp3 amorphous carbon in its structure (Guo et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019). The ratios of the intensity of the G peak to that of the D peak (IG /ID ) for lignin, C4 and C5 were 0.65, 0.38 and 0.54, respectively, indicating that the graphitization of lignin decreased after acidolysis (Ding et al., 2018a; Zheng et al., 2021). The Raman spectra of C1, C2, and C3 show no distinct D and G peaks, while two weak peaks appear at 1306 and 1112 cm−1, which may be due to the CC bond stretching and torsion modes in their structures. This suggests that the solid residue components C4 and C5 mainly inherit the macromolecular carbon structure of lignin, whereas the supernatant components C1–C3 most likely originate from the branched chains on the C9 unit of lignin. As shown in Fig. 3b, the surface functional groups of lignin and different components were characterized by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra of lignin, C4 and C5 show relatively similar peak patterns, while the surface structures of C1–C3 are obviously richer than those of lignin, C4 and C5. The broad peaks at 3072–3527 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O-H groups, while the peaks at 3329, 2933, 1723, 1607, 1321 and 1140 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of N-H, C-H, CO, CC, C-N and C-O groups, respectively (Jia et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2017). Notably, the vibrational peaks of the N-H and C-N groups in the FT-IR spectra of C1–C3 are significantly stronger than those of the C4 and C5 components. As shown in Fig. 3c, the XPS survey spectra of lignin and different components show distinct peaks at 284.8 eV (C1s) and 532.7 eV (O1s), and there are evident peaks at 400.1 eV (N1s) for C1–C3. This indicates that the surfaces of lignin, C4 and C5 are mainly composed of C and O, while C1–C3 are mainly composed of C, O and N, which is consistent with the FT-IR results.

The chemical compositions of lignin and different components were further analyzed by high-resolution XPS. As shown in Fig. 3d, C 1s can be fitted to three peaks at 288.7, 286.3, and 284.8 eV, which correspond to CO, sp3 carbon (C-O/C-N), and sp2 carbon (C-C/CC), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3e, O 1s can be fitted to two peaks at 532.7 and 531.5 eV, corresponding to C-O-C/C-OH and CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3f, N 1s can be fitted to two peaks at 401.8 and 399.4 eV, corresponding to graphitic nitrogen (C-N) and amide nitrogen (N-H), respectively (Jia et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021b). The results of the quantitative analysis of the XPS data for lignin and different lignin components are listed in Table 1 . The C content of lignin decreases from 62.65 % to 48.68 % to 57.15 % after acidolysis, while the O content increases from 37.35 % to 43.20 % as the N content increases from 0.13 % to 12.40 %. The N-H contents of C1–C3 were 11.07 %, 10.54 % and 8.12 %, respectively, while the N-H contents of C4 and C5 were significantly reduced to 0.19 % and 0.13 %, respectively. The C-O-C/C-OH contents of C4 and C5 (38.64 %–40.51 %) were significantly greater than those of C1–C3 (9.71 %∼22.58 %), which may be due to the oxidation of lignin during acidolysis. According to the above characterization results, it can be reasonably concluded that the surfaces of components C1–C3 after the acidolysis of lignin are rich in N-H and C-OH functional groups, while components C4 and C5 mainly inherit the aromatic structure of lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CQDs from components of lignin acidolysis
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, size and molecular weight distribution of the CQDs (CQDs-1–CQDs-5) derived from different components were characterized using TEM, HR-TEM and HR-MS. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the average particle sizes of CQDs-1–CQDs-5 were 4.5, 4.3, 4.1, 4.9, and 5.1 nm, respectively, indicating that the sizes of the CQDs derived from the supernatant of the acidolysis lignin were smaller than those derived from the solid residue. HR-TEM images show that all five CQDs have a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm inside the carbon core, similar to the crystalline structure of graphitic carbon (002) (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2016). Notably, the CQD-1–CQD-3 carbon cores show a good interior hexagonal crystal surface, similar to the graphene quantum dot structure. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Mn values of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are 370, 530, and 650, respectively, which are consistent with the molecular weight distribution trend of precursors C1–C3, which exhibit a gradual increasing trend. The Mn values of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 are close to 450 and 410, respectively.

The optical properties, including the absorption capacity, emission spectra and fluorescence lifetime, of the five CQDs were analyzed using UV‒vis, PL and TRPL (Time-resolved Photoluminescence). As shown in Fig. 4c, the CQD-1–CQD-3 aqueous solutions emit bright green fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm, and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. CQD-4 and CQD-5 aqueous solutions emit blue fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity of CQD-4 is significantly weaker than that of CQD-5. The UV‒vis spectra of all five CQDs show significant absorption peaks in the range of 200–300 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C in the carbon core (Wang et al., 2021a; Zhao et al., 2021). The absorption decreases gradually from CQDs-1 to CQDs-3, while it increases gradually from CQDs-4 to CQDs-5 in the range of 200–300 nm. CQDs-1–CQDs-3 show weak absorption peaks at 300–500 nm, which are attributed to the n-π* transition of C=O or C=N on the surface of the CQDs. The absorption edges of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are ∼∼500 nm, while those of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 are–400 nm, and the enhanced light absorption in the visible region facilitates the long-wavelength emission of CQDs-1–CQDs-3. The PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths show that the maximum emission wavelengths of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are 475 nm, and the emission peak positions gradually redshift with increasing excitation wavelength, demonstrating excitation-dependent behavior. The maximum emission wavelength of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 is 400 nm, and the peak positions of the emission spectra are almost unchanged with increasing excitation wavelength, resulting in the excitation-independent behavior of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5. A number of studies have indicated that excitation-independent PL behavior is usually associated with fewer surface defects (Zhu et al., 2022). It is noteworthy that the five lignin-derived CQDs display negligible variation in their PL emission behavior at room temperature and under UV light irradiation, and almost no photofading occurs in Fig. S2 (see supplementary materials).

As shown in Fig. 4d, the TRPL decay curves of CQDs-1–CQDs-5 can all be fitted as biexponentials, whereas the TRPL decay times of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are significantly longer than those of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5. The fitting parameters of the TRPL decay curves for the different CQDs are listed in Table 2 . It includes the τ 1 component associated with the eigenstates and the τ 2 component associated with the surface defect states. The former originates from π-π* transitions in the sp2 conjugated domains within the carbon core of CQDs, while the latter results from n-π* transitions of O- or N-containing functional groups on the surface (Zhao et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2021). The average lifetimes (τ avg) of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are 5.34, 5.19, 5.90, and 5.09 ns, with A1 values of 67.82 %, 68.15 %, and 69.02 %, respectively. This demonstrates that the luminescence behavior of the CQDs derived from the supernatant after lignin acidolysis is quite similar and is synergistically affected by both the carbon core eigenstate and the surface defect state. The τ avg values of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 were 3.33 and 3.26 ns, with A1 values of 89.84 % and 91.14 %, respectively. This indicates that the PL behavior of the CQDs derived from the solid residue after lignin acidolysis is quite similar and dominated by carbon-core eigenstates. Additionally, the QYs and yields of the CQDs derived from the lignin acidolysis components are shown in Table 3 . CQD-1 simultaneously has the highest QYs (19.5 %) and Yields (16.5 %), and the QYs and Yields of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are much greater than those of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5. In conclusion, C1 in the supernatant after lignin acidolysis is the key precursor for lignin-derived CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characteristics of the CQDs from components of lignin acidolysis
ce:section.ce:para: The structural properties of the CQDs from components of lignin acidolysis were analyzed using Raman spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the Raman spectra of all five CQDs show two distinct peaks at 1368 and 1587 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G peaks, respectively. The G peak represents sp2 graphitic carbon in the structure of the CQDs, while the D peak represents sp3 amorphous carbon in the structure of the CQDs. The ratio of the intensity of the G peak to that of the D peak (IG /ID ) indicates the degree of graphitization of the CQDs, and the IG/ID values of CQDs-1–CQDs-5 are 1.16, 1.17, 1.14, 0.98 and 1.02, respectively. This demonstrates that the graphitization of CQDs-1 to CQDs-3 derived from the supernatant after lignin acidolysis is greater than that of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 derived from the solid residue. As shown in Fig. 5b, the surface functional groups of the five CQDs derived from the lignin acidolysis components were characterized by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs-1 to CQDs-3 derived from the supernatant after lignin acidolysis are similar, whereas those of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 derived from the solid residue are comparable. The surface structures of CQDs-1 to CQDs-3 are more abundant than those of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5. The broad peak at 3462 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the O-H group, while the peaks at 3244, 2951, 1731, 1602, 1319, and 1010 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of the N-H, C-H, CO, CC, C-N, and C-O groups, respectively. Among them, the vibrational peaks of the C-N groups in the FT-IR spectra of CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are significantly stronger than those of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5, and the N-H groups of C1–C3 are gradually transformed into C-N groups of CQDs-1–CQDs-3. As shown in Fig. 5c, the XPS survey spectra of the five CQDs derived from the lignin acidolysis components show distinct peaks at 284.8 eV (C1s) and 532.7 eV (O1s), and there is a distinct peak at 400.1 eV (N1s) for CQDs-1–CQDs-3. This indicates that CQDs-1–CQDs-3 are mainly composed of C, O and N, and the surfaces of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 are mainly composed of C and O, which is consistent with the FT-IR results.

The chemical compositions of the five CQDs were further analyzed using high-resolution XPS. As shown in Fig. 5d, C 1 s can be fitted into three peaks at 288.9, 286.3, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to CO, sp3 carbon (C-O/C-N), and sp2 carbon (C-C/CC), respectively. As shown in Fig. 5e, O 1 s can be fitted to two peaks at 533.2 and 531.9 eV, corresponding to C-O-C/C-OH and CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5f, N 1s can be fitted to two peaks at 401.8 and 399.5 eV, corresponding to graphitic nitrogen (C-N) and amide nitrogen (N-H), respectively. The quantitative XPS analysis data of the CQDs are listed in Table 4 . Compared with that of the lignin acetolysis components C1–C5 (Table 1), the C content of CQDs-1–CQDs-5 increases significantly, while the O content decreases. In particular, the N-H content of CQDs-1 to CQDs-3 decreases sharply, and the C-N content increases significantly, whereas the N content of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 changes slightly compared to that of C4 and C5. This indicates that the ability of the lignin acidolysis components C1–C3 to form CQDs-1–CQDs-3 likely underwent aminodehydration condensation and carbonation reactions, and these pathways are quite different from those of C4 and C5 for the formation of CQDs-4 and CQDs-5.

The evolution of the 13C–1H chemical structures of lignin, acidolysis components C1–C5 and their derived CQDs-1–CQDs-5 were analyzed using 2D-HSQC NMR. Lignin is a complex, amorphous, three-dimensional polymer consisting mainly of three types of phenylpropane units linked by ether or C-C bonds (Zhu et al., 2019a; Zhu et al., 2019b). As shown in Fig. 6 a, the chemical shift of the 2D-HSQC NMR spectrum is 1H/13C ppm, including two regions, the aliphatic region (δC/δH: 50–90/3.2–5.5 ppm) and the aromatic region (δC/δH: 100–135/6.0–7.8 ppm). In the aliphatic region, the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of lignin exhibit abundant signals at 55.6/3.7, 60.5–82.5/3.3–4.8, 54.1–56.2/3.4–3.6, and 55.4–84.9/3.4–4.3 ppm, which are attributed to the natural structure of lignin in the methoxy (OMe), β-aryl ether (A), phenylcoumarin (B) and resinol (C) units of the natural structure of lignin, respectively. In the aromatic region, the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of lignin show signals at 110.2–126.7/7.1, 126.1/7.1, 132.9/7.1, 114.9–120.9/6.7, and 120.9/7.5 ppm, which correspond to the natural structure of lignin in the ferulic acid ester (FA), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), guaiacol (G) and oxidized guaiacol (G') units in the natural structure of lignin. There was almost no detectable signal from the lilac (S) and p-coumarin-based (pCA) structural units (Wang et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 6b, the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of the components (C1–C5) of AL subjected to low-temperature acidolysis show a significantly reduced signal in the aliphatic region of the A, B and C structural units. In the aliphatic region, the proportions of β-O-4, β-5, and β-β linkages in the lignin and acidolysis components can be obtained by integrating over the areas of the linkage signals (A, B, and C). As shown in Table 5 , the β-5 linkage bond integral area (13–19) and β-β (5–7) in the C1–C3 supernatant fractions of lignin sharply decreased after low-temperature acidolysis. In particular, the β-O-4 linkage bond signal is weak and almost negligible. The solid residue components C4 and C5 after acidolysis show similar results to the signal integration results of the three linkages in the lignin structure, containing mainly β-O-4 (10–11), β-5 (16–22), and a small amount of β-β linkages (6–8). In the aromatic region, the C1–C3 in the lignin supernatant after low-temperature acidolysis contain mainly G (34–36) and H (31 %∼35 %) structural units, as well as the newly appearing pCA (31–33) structural unit. Lignin and the acidolysis components C4 and C5 contain a large number of FA (6–24), G (78–82) and H (20) structural units. As shown in Fig. 6c, the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of CQDs-1–CQDs-5 derived from lignin acidolysis components show signals at 3.5 ppm in the aliphatic region corresponding to the carbonyl-C=O (Cbl)-containing side chain structure on the surface of the CQDs. The integral area of the Cbl linkage bond decreases sequentially from 22 for CQDs-1 to 5 for CQDs-5. In the aromatic region, the signals at 6.5 and 7.6 ppm correspond to pyridine (Pyd) and pyrrole (Pyl) aromatic structures, respectively (Yang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). From CQDs-1 to CQDs-3, the integral areas of the Pyd and Pyl structural units gradually decrease from 61 and 63 to 17 and 11, respectively. In contrast, CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 consist mainly of aromatic structural units of FA (12–18), G (39–48) and H (10–11) in the original structure of lignin. These results indicate that the ether bond β-O-4 in the lignin structure breaks significantly under low-temperature acidolysis, and the reactive organic small molecules are mainly present in the supernatant components C1–C3. The further hydrothermally formed CQDs-1–CQDs-3 mainly contain pyridine and pyrrole aromatic structural units. Most of the lignin fragments after acidolysis exist in the solid residue components C4 and C5, and the derived CQDs-4 and CQDs-5 retain the main aromatic structural units of FA, H and G of lignin, which is in good agreement with the results of the FTIR and XPS analyses described above.

On the basis of these experimental results, possible pathways for the two-step synthesis of lignin-derived CQDs are proposed. As shown in Fig. 7 , during the first step of the low-temperature acidolysis process, the β-aryl-ether bond in the lignin polymer is readily cleaved to undergo deetherization and depolymerization reactions. The intermediates from lignin acidolysis are structurally complex and diverse, consisting mainly of the supernatant and solid residue. The water-soluble supernatant component consists mainly of organic small molecule fragments from lignin depolymerization, while most of the lignin fragments remain in the solid residue component. Notably, 2-aminobenzenesulfonic acid plays a triple role in catalyzing lignin depolymerization, N-element doping and surface activation during acidolysis. In the second step of the hydrothermal process, the organic small molecules in the supernatant fraction as precursors contain abundant –COOH, –OH and –NH2 groups on their surfaces. Inter- and intramolecular amine dehydration condensation and hydroxyl aldehyde condensation reactions occur under high temperature and pressure, and N atoms are simultaneously doped into the carbon skeleton in the form of C-N. Further polymerization, cross-linking and carbonization with prolonged hydrothermal carbonization time form conjugated molecules, and rapid π-π stacking and sp2 hybridization lead to the growth of carbon nuclei and ultimately to the formation of CQDs. Briefly, the lignin feedstock first undergoes top-down depolymerization in the presence of acid, and then the supernatant component undergoes bottom-up assembly in the reaction system to form the CQDs product. In contrast, the solid residue component from the oxidation of lignin during acidolysis retains the main bulk of the carbon skeleton of lignin and is structurally defective by the introduction of epoxy groups on the basal plane. These activated large sp2 carbon domains in the solid residue component undergo hydrothermal cleavage after prolonged exposure to high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, followed by deoxygenation and eventual decomposition into small carbon domain CQDs products.

A series of lignin-derived CQDs were prepared via a two-step low-temperature acidolysis-hydrothermal method. The surfaces of supernatant components C1–C3 are rich in N-H and C-OH reactive groups, which corresponding CQDs-1–CQDs-3 emit bright green fluorescence. In the first step of the low-temperature acidolysis process, the lignin polymer first undergoes deethering and depolymerization reactions. During the hydrothermal process in the second step, the organic small molecules on the surface of the supernatant are enriched with reactive groups that are dehydrated, condensed, crosslinked, and carbonized under high temperature and pressure to form CQDs.

Lingli Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hao Wu: Resources. Hongmin Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Dekui Shen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Haitao Hu: Visualization, Formal analysis. Mengjia Dou: Software.

Dekui Shen reports financial support was provided by Key Research & Development Program of Jiangsu Province. Dekui Shen reports financial support was provided by Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province from the Education Department of Jiangsu.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 52376172 and 52406250), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant BK20240592), the College Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant 24KJB480011), the Key Research & Development Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant BE2020114) and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Extreme Conditions (Grant 2023B1212010002). The authors would like to thank Zhao Jie from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TPRL analysis.
